title: Women at the Helm: Redefining India’s Development Story
content:
India’s development narrative has decisively shifted from empowering women to being powered by women. From classrooms to cutting-edge research labs, women are claiming their space in STEM at an unprecedented pace, with 43% enrolment and over 53% UGC-NET fellowships. Female enrolment in higher education has surged from 1.57 crore to 2.18 crore, while doctoral enrolment has grown by over 135% in under a decade. As India marches toward Viksit Bharat 2047, Nari Shakti is no longer a welfare goal but the driving force of the nation’s economy.

Key Strides Towards Women-Led Development

Political Empowerment: The Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (2023) marks a historic step by ensuring 33% reservation for women in Parliament and State Assemblies. With over 14.5 lakh elected women representatives in Panchayati Raj Institutions, India is strengthening grassroots democracy and moving beyond symbolic participation.

Rural Economic Transformation: The Lakhpati Didi initiative is revolutionizing rural livelihoods by enabling women-led SHGs to participate in high-value sectors like drone technology, organic farming, and renewable energy. Over 2 crore women have already achieved annual incomes above ₹1 lakh, with a target of 3 crore Lakhpati Didis.

STEM & Innovation Leadership: Initiatives like WISE-KIRAN and Vigyan Jyoti are bridging gender gaps in science. Women now account for 18.6% of India’s R&D workforce, contributing to innovation and the Fourth Industrial Revolution.

Financial Inclusion & Digital Power: Through PM Jan Dhan Yojana and GeM Womaniya, women now hold 56% of bank accounts, gaining financial independence, improved credit access, and entry into national markets through MSMEs.

Rising Workforce Participation: Female Labour Force Participation Rate has shown a structural rise, reaching 35.3% in 2025, driven by rural employment, entrepreneurship, and better workplace support systems.

Leadership in Defense: Women’s participation in the armed forces has increased from 3,000 to over 11,000 officers, with entry into combat roles and institutions like the NDA signaling a gender-neutral approach to national security.

Urban Mobility & Inclusion: Initiatives like Pink Buses and PM-eBus Sewa are creating safer transport systems while generating employment. Over 5,100 women are now engaged in toll and transport operations across India.

Healthcare & Welfare: Under Mission Shakti 2.0, a life-cycle approach ensures better maternal health, nutrition, and financial security, reducing economic barriers caused by health issues.

Key Challenges Hindering Progress

Time Poverty & Unpaid Care: Women perform nearly 84% of unpaid care work, contributing an estimated 15–17% of GDP that remains invisible, limiting their participation in leadership roles.

Limited Access to Capital: Women entrepreneurs face systemic barriers, receiving only ₹4 out of every ₹100 in funding, restricting their ability to scale businesses.

Leaky Pipeline & Pay Gap: Despite high education levels, women face drop-offs in mid-career stages due to the motherhood penalty, with India ranked 131st in the Gender Gap Index 2025.

Implementation Gaps: Social issues like the Sarpanch-Pati phenomenon reduce real decision-making power for elected women, limiting true grassroots empowerment.

Climate Burden: Women in rural areas face a triple burden of farm work, domestic responsibilities, and resource management. While 85% are engaged in agriculture, only 13% own land.

Informal Workforce Trap: Nearly 94% of working women are in the informal sector, facing low wages and lack of social security.

Digital Risks & AI Bias: Rising cyber harassment, deepfakes, and underrepresentation in AI (22%) limit women’s participation in the digital economy.

Way Forward

India must adopt gender-responsive urban planning with safer infrastructure and care-centered design. Expanding SHE-Mart cooperatives will help women scale businesses from local to global markets.

Introducing returnship programs can address the leaky pipeline by supporting women re-entering the workforce. Investments in climate-resilient agri-tech like Drone Didi and women-led farming models will strengthen rural economies.

Building care economy infrastructure such as childcare centers can reduce unpaid work burdens, while digital safety frameworks are essential to protect women in online spaces.

Women-led development is not just a policy vision—it is the foundation of India’s inclusive and sustainable future.

title: India as a Global Peacemaker in a Polarized World Order
content:
The recent escalation in West Asia, particularly the US–Israel strikes on Iran, highlights the return of hard power politics in global affairs. In an increasingly polarized world, the need for credible mediators has never been greater. With its doctrine of strategic autonomy, balanced diplomacy, and trust across the Global South, India is emerging as a potential global peacemaker capable of reducing geopolitical tensions.

India as a Stabilizing Global Force

Voice of the Global South: India has positioned itself as a strong advocate for developing nations, countering both Western financial dominance and China’s debt diplomacy. During its G20 presidency, India successfully integrated the African Union, amplifying the voice of over 1.4 billion people and promoting a more inclusive global order.

Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI): India is exporting its India Stack (UPI, Aadhaar) as an open, non-exploitative digital model. With UPI linkages in countries like UAE, Nepal, and Namibia and initiatives like Vaccine Maitri, India is strengthening its human-centric soft power.

Strategic Balance in West Asia: India practices “strategic de-hyphenation”, maintaining strong ties with Israel, Iran, and Saudi Arabia. This unique position allows India to act as a neutral mediator during regional conflicts while safeguarding its strategic interests.

Eurasian Mediation Role: By maintaining relations with both Russia and Ukraine, India has emerged as a credible back-channel mediator. Its policy of not weaponizing trade enables it to build trust across conflicting blocs.

Indo-Pacific Security Provider: India plays a key role in maintaining maritime stability through partnerships like the Quad and exercises such as Malabar, ensuring secure trade routes and deterring unilateral aggression.

Supply Chain Stabilizer: Through initiatives like PLI schemes, India is emerging as a trusted manufacturing hub. Global firms like Apple are shifting production to India, reducing dependence on unstable supply chains.

Climate Diplomacy Leader: India bridges the gap between developed and developing nations through initiatives like the International Solar Alliance and advocacy for climate equity, preventing climate issues from becoming geopolitical conflicts.

Humanitarian Leadership: Operations like Operation Dost and Operation Sadbhav demonstrate India’s role as a first responder, building trust through humanitarian assistance.

Key Constraints

Geopolitical Pressure: India faces pressure from global powers to align, challenging its strategic neutrality.

Regional Tensions: Conflicts with China and Pakistan limit India’s ability to focus on global mediation.

Diplomatic Capacity: India’s diplomatic network remains relatively under-resourced compared to its global ambitions.

Institutional Limitations: Lack of a permanent seat in the UN Security Council restricts its formal influence.

Economic Constraints: Balancing domestic development with global commitments creates a “growth vs geopolitics” dilemma.

Energy Dependence: Heavy reliance on imports, especially through the Strait of Hormuz, complicates neutral diplomacy.

Defense Dependency: As one of the largest arms importers, India faces strategic vulnerabilities in asserting independent positions.

Way Forward

Expand Diplomatic Capacity: Strengthening the Indian Foreign Service and building specialized mediation units.

Institutionalize Mediation: Creating formal conflict resolution frameworks and peace envoy systems.

Defense & Tech Indigenization: Reducing external dependence to enhance strategic autonomy.

Humanitarian Leadership: Expanding HADR capabilities to act as a global security provider.

Minilateral Coalitions: Leading flexible alliances on issues like climate, cybersecurity, and maritime security.

Future Norm Setting: Shaping global rules in AI, space, and emerging technologies.

Diplomatic Philosophy: Leveraging India’s ethos of pluralism and non-violence to offer an alternative global model.

India’s “middle path” diplomacy positions it as a key architect of a stable, multipolar world order in an era dominated by hard power.

title: End-of-Life Rights in India: Law, Ethics, and Human Dignity
content:
India’s approach to passive euthanasia reflects a careful balance between constitutional values, medical ethics, and human dignity. Through landmark judgments like Aruna Shanbaug (2011) and Common Cause (2018), the Supreme Court recognized the Right to Die with Dignity under Article 21. Recent rulings have further strengthened safeguards, ensuring that end-of-life care remains compassionate, ethical, and legally secure.

Understanding Euthanasia

Euthanasia refers to intentionally ending life to relieve severe pain or suffering in terminal illness.

Passive Euthanasia: Withdrawing life support such as ventilators — Legal in India (with safeguards).

Active Euthanasia: Deliberately causing death through lethal means — Illegal in India.

Judicial Evolution

P. Rathinam (1994): Recognized Right to Die under Article 21.

Gian Kaur (1996): Rejected this view, stating life cannot include the right to end it.

Aruna Shanbaug (2011): Allowed passive euthanasia under strict safeguards, requiring High Court approval and medical board review.

Common Cause (2018): Established Right to Die with Dignity and legalized Living Wills.

Recent Developments: Courts have emphasized palliative care and patient dignity, ensuring decisions are made in the best interest of the patient.

Current Procedure

The withdrawal of life support follows a structured process:

• Verification of Living Will and patient condition
• Formation of Primary and Secondary Medical Boards
• Consent from nominee or family
• Final documentation and legal oversight

This ensures transparency and protection against misuse.

Arguments in Favour

Bodily Autonomy: Individuals have the right to refuse treatment.

Dignified Death: Prevents prolonged suffering in terminal illness.

Living Wills: Empower patients to make future medical decisions.

Economic Relief: Reduces catastrophic healthcare costs for families.

Arguments Against

Risk of Misuse: Economic pressure may influence decisions.

Medical Uncertainty: Possibility of misdiagnosis or recovery.

Ethical Concerns: Challenges the sanctity of life principle.

Slippery Slope: May expand beyond terminal illness cases.

Way Forward

Legal Framework: Enact a comprehensive End-of-Life Care law.

Digital Living Wills: Integrate with ABHA system for easy access.

Palliative Care: Ensure mandatory comfort care pathways.

Ethics Committees: Establish district-level oversight bodies.

Awareness: Promote death literacy and informed decision-making.

Passive euthanasia in India marks a shift from preserving life at all costs to preserving dignity at life’s end.

title: Beyond Calories: India’s Shift Towards Nutritional Security
content:
While the Green Revolution helped India overcome mass starvation, it largely focused on caloric sufficiency rather than nutritional quality. Today, India faces a crisis of “hidden hunger”, where diets provide enough calories but lack essential micronutrients. Ensuring nutritional security is now critical for improving health, productivity, and the nation’s human capital.

Current Status of Nutritional Security

Undernourishment: Nearly 12% of India’s population (~172 million people) remains undernourished, with India ranking 102nd in the Global Hunger Index 2025.

Child Malnutrition: 35% children are stunted and 18.7% are wasted, while rising obesity reflects a double burden of malnutrition.

Micronutrient Deficiency: High anemia levels persist, affecting 67.1% of children and 59.1% of adolescent girls, indicating widespread hidden hunger.

Diet Affordability: Around 40.4% of people cannot afford a healthy diet, with inflation making nutrient-rich food inaccessible.

Key Developments

Biofortified Crops: Over 150 nutrient-rich crop varieties have been developed, improving iron, zinc, and protein content.

Rice Fortification: The Public Distribution System now delivers fortified rice nationwide, helping reduce anemia levels.

Digital Governance: The Poshan Tracker ensures real-time monitoring and targeted delivery of nutrition services.

Millet Revolution: Promotion of “Shree Anna” (millets) is improving dietary diversity and climate resilience.

First 1000 Days Focus: Programs like PMMVY 2.0 emphasize maternal and child nutrition during critical early stages.

Climate-Smart Agriculture: Policies promote nutrition-per-drop farming and sustainable crop systems.

Key Challenges

Calorie-Nutrient Mismatch: Food systems remain focused on cereals, leading to protein and micronutrient deficiency.

Persistent Malnutrition: High stunting and wasting reflect intergenerational poverty and poor maternal health.

Anemia in Women: Gender inequality results in chronic iron deficiency among women.

Economic Barriers: Fruits, vegetables, and protein sources remain unaffordable for many households.

Climate Impact: Rising CO₂ levels are reducing nutrient quality in staple crops.

Double Burden: India faces both undernutrition and rising obesity/NCDs simultaneously.

Awareness Gap: Lack of nutritional literacy leads to poor dietary choices and reliance on processed foods.

Way Forward

Nutrition-Linked MSP: Incentivize farmers to grow nutrient-rich crops.

Diverse Food Basket: Expand PDS to include millets, pulses, and local superfoods.

First 1000 Days Focus: Strengthen convergence of health, nutrition, and sanitation.

Digital Targeting: Use AI and data systems to identify nutrition hotspots.

Community Participation: Promote Poshan Vatikas and local accountability systems.

Fortification: Expand mandatory fortification of oil, milk, and salt.

Awareness Campaigns: Encourage dietary diversity and healthy eating habits.

India’s challenge is no longer feeding its population, but nourishing it. A shift to nutrition security is essential for building a healthier and more productive future.

title: From Access to Equity: Transforming India’s Healthcare System
content:
India’s healthcare landscape is undergoing a transformation from limited reach to broad-based accessibility and affordability. Despite progress, high out-of-pocket expenditure (~39%) continues to challenge equitable access. Initiatives like Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY and Jan Aushadhi Pariyojana are expanding financial protection and affordable medicine access, marking a shift toward people-centric universal healthcare.

Strengthening Access and Equity

Digital Health Ecosystem: The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) has created over 79 crore ABHA IDs, enabling interoperable health records and seamless care across regions.

Universal Health Coverage: PM-JAY, the world’s largest public insurance scheme, provides cashless treatment and has issued 43 crore cards, covering vulnerable populations including senior citizens.

Telemedicine Expansion: Platforms like eSanjeevani have delivered over 43 crore consultations, bridging rural-urban healthcare gaps.

Affordable Medicines: Over 18,000 Jan Aushadhi Kendras provide medicines at 50–80% lower prices, reducing financial burden.

Primary Healthcare Boost: Ayushman Arogya Mandirs are strengthening grassroots care with focus on prevention, wellness, and NCD screening.

Workforce Expansion: Medical colleges have increased from 387 to 800+, along with expansion of AIIMS institutions, improving doctor availability.

AI & Health-Tech: AI-driven tools and digital platforms are enhancing diagnostics, surveillance, and decision-making.

Immunization Digitization: The U-WIN platform tracks vaccination of 3.7 crore pregnant women and 11 crore infants annually.

Persisting Challenges

High Out-of-Pocket Costs: Despite schemes, healthcare expenses still push millions into medical poverty.

Urban-Rural Divide: Nearly 70% healthcare infrastructure is concentrated in urban areas serving only 30% population.

Human Resource Gap: Severe shortage of specialists in rural areas weakens healthcare delivery.

Digital Divide: Limited access to technology restricts benefits of digital health initiatives.

Low Public Spending: Health expenditure remains below optimal levels, limiting system capacity.

Quality & Regulation Issues: Weak oversight leads to malpractices and antimicrobial resistance concerns.

Supply Chain Gaps: Frequent drug shortages undermine public healthcare reliability.

NCD Burden: Non-communicable diseases account for over 60% of deaths, straining the system.

Way Forward

Increase Health Financing: Expand public investment through dedicated health funding mechanisms.

Strengthen Primary Care: Focus on preventive and community-based healthcare.

Expand Insurance Coverage: Include outpatient care and diagnostics to reduce OOPE.

Improve Regulation: Establish strong oversight to ensure quality and affordability.

Leverage Technology: Use AI and digital tools for last-mile healthcare delivery.

Public-Private Partnerships: Expand infrastructure in underserved areas through PPP models.

Achieving universal healthcare in India requires a shift from reactive treatment to preventive, inclusive, and digitally integrated care.

title: Tourism in India: Unlocking a $1 Trillion Growth Engine
content:
Tourism remains one of India’s most underexploited growth engines, contributing 6.7% to GDP and employing nearly 48 million people. With every rupee invested generating 3.5 times more jobs than the average sector, tourism offers unmatched employment potential. Despite having 44 UNESCO World Heritage Sites, India attracts only 10 million foreign tourists, highlighting a vast untapped opportunity. Strategic scaling can transform tourism into a $1 trillion sector by 2035.

Significance of Tourism in India

Economic Growth Engine: Tourism acts as a high-multiplier sector, boosting industries like hospitality, transport, and construction, with projections estimating ₹42 trillion contribution by 2035.

Mass Employment Generator: It provides inclusive employment for youth, women, and rural communities, reducing disguised unemployment.

Foreign Exchange Source: Tourism acts as an “invisible export”, generating over $35 billion in forex earnings.

Infrastructure Catalyst: Initiatives like UDAN and Vande Bharat trains are improving connectivity to key destinations.

Soft Power & Cultural Promotion: Tourism strengthens Brand India globally through heritage, culture, and events like G20.

Medical & Wellness Tourism: India is emerging as a hub for affordable healthcare and AYUSH-based wellness.

Sustainable Growth: Programs like Swadesh Darshan 2.0 promote eco-friendly and responsible tourism.

Regional Development: Tourism drives rural development through homestays and local entrepreneurship.

Key Challenges

Infrastructure Gaps: Poor last-mile connectivity reduces tourist comfort and accessibility.

Overtourism: Popular destinations face ecological stress and overcrowding.

High Costs: Elevated GST and pricing reduce global competitiveness.

Safety Concerns: Perception of unsafe travel, especially for women, impacts tourism inflows.

Waste Management: Poor systems lead to environmental degradation in tourist areas.

Skill Deficit: Lack of trained workforce affects service quality.

Policy & Licensing Issues: Complex regulations discourage small businesses.

Fragmented Branding: Lack of a unified tourism identity weakens global positioning.

Way Forward

Digital Integration: Develop a Unified Tourism Platform for seamless travel experiences.

Infrastructure Status: Grant full status to hospitality to boost investment.

Sustainable Tourism: Implement carrying capacity-based regulations.

Safety Measures: Deploy tourism police and emergency systems.

Tax Reforms: Rationalize GST to improve price competitiveness.

Skill Development: Invest in hospitality training and experience-based services.

Improve Connectivity: Strengthen last-mile transport infrastructure.

Promote Niche Tourism: Focus on MICE, wedding, eco and astro-tourism.

Tourism has the potential to become a cornerstone of India’s economic growth, job creation, and global influence if developed in a sustainable and strategic manner.
